revision.comment: initial text of a stub article -- 1st steps in a constructive direction
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kibune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kibune Jinja}}, also known as '''Kifune Jinja''', is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]] in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]].
The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kibune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
== See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kibune Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]]
{{Shinto-stub}}
[[ja:貴船神社]]

revision.text:
[[File:Kibune Shrine.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Kibune Shrine in Kyoto.]] {{nihongo|'''Kibune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kibune Jinja}}, also known as '''Kifune Jinja''', is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]] in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kibune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kibune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines. <ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
== See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]

==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>
== See also == * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== * [http://kifunejinja.jp/ Official Site] * {{Twitter}}

These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>
== See also == * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== * [http://kifunejinja.jp/ Official Site] * {{Twitter}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>
== See also == * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== * [http://kifunejinja.jp/ Official Site] * {{Twitter}}

==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>
== See also == * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. {{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = Kifune-jinja haiden.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|07|18|N|135|45|46|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Kuraokami]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:貴船神社]][[en:Kifune Shrine]][[fr:Kifune-jinja]][[ja:貴船神社]][[ko:기후네 신사]][[my:ခိဖုနဲနတ်ကျောင်း]][[ru:Кифунэ-дзиндзя]][[zh:貴船神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q276779]] Wikidata name: Kifune Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q276779]] Wikidata name: Kifune Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.12166667|N|135.76277778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Kuraokami|qid=Q644201}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|qid=Q1321832}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|qid=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320}} }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[:en:Sandō|Sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[:en:Hashihime|Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[:en:Oni|Oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>
== See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== * [http://kifunejinja.jp/ Official Site] * {{Twitter}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[:en:Hashihime|Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[:en:Oni|Oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

[[en:Kifune Shrine]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:貴船神社]][[en:Kifune Shrine]][[fr:Kifune-jinja]][[ja:貴船神社]][[ko:기후네 신사]][[my:ခိဖုနဲနတ်ကျောင်း]][[ru:Кифунэ-дзиндзя]][[zh:貴船神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q276779]] Wikidata name: Kifune Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q276779]] Wikidata name: Kifune Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.12166667|N|135.76277778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[:en:Sandō|Sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[:en:Hashihime|Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[:en:Oni|Oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

(1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:貴船神社]][[en:Kifune Shrine]][[fr:Kifune-jinja]][[ja:貴船神社]][[ko:기후네 신사]][[my:ခိဖုနဲနတ်ကျောင်း]][[ru:Кифунэ-дзиндзя]][[zh:貴船神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:貴船神社]][[en:Kifune Shrine]][[fr:Kifune-jinja]][[ja:貴船神社]][[ko:기후네 신사]][[my:ခိဖုနဲနတ်ကျောင်း]][[ru:Кифунэ-дзиндзя]][[zh:貴船神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q276779]] Wikidata name: Kifune Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q276779]] Wikidata name: Kifune Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.12166667|N|135.76277778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[:en:Sandō|Sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[:en:Hashihime|Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[:en:Oni|Oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>
== See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:貴船神社]][[en:Kifune Shrine]][[fr:Kifune-jinja]][[ja:貴船神社]][[ko:기후네 신사]][[my:ခိဖုနဲနတ်ကျောင်း]][[ru:Кифунэ-дзиндзя]][[zh:貴船神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:貴船神社]][[en:Kifune Shrine]][[fr:Kifune-jinja]][[ja:貴船神社]][[ko:기후네 신사]][[my:ခိဖုနဲနတ်ကျောင်း]][[ru:Кифунэ-дзиндзя]][[zh:貴船神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:貴船神社]][[en:Kifune Shrine]][[fr:Kifune-jinja]][[ja:貴船神社]][[ko:기후네 신사]][[my:ခိဖုနဲနတ်ကျောင်း]][[ru:Кифунэ-дзиндзя]][[zh:貴船神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q276779]] Wikidata name: Kifune Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q276779]] Wikidata name: Kifune Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.12166667|N|135.76277778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''[[:en:Sandō|Sandō]]'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that [[:en:Hashihime|Hashihime]] (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''[[:en:Oni|Oni]]'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that {{ill|Hashihime|en|Hashihime|WD=Q3783599|lt=Hashihime}} (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni}}'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh}} play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>
== See also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]

==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
