revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:貴船神社]][[en:Kifune Shrine]][[fr:Kifune-jinja]][[ja:貴船神社]][[ko:기후네 신사]][[my:ခိဖုနဲနတ်ကျောင်း]][[ru:Кифунэ-дзиндзя]][[zh:貴船神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:貴船神社]][[en:Kifune Shrine]][[fr:Kifune-jinja]][[ja:貴船神社]][[ko:기후네 신사]][[my:ခိဖုနဲနတ်ကျောင်း]][[ru:Кифунэ-дзиндзя]][[zh:貴船神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:貴船神社]][[en:Kifune Shrine]][[fr:Kifune-jinja]][[ja:貴船神社]][[ko:기후네 신사]][[my:ခိဖုနဲနတ်ကျောင်း]][[ru:Кифунэ-дзиндзя]][[zh:貴船神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q276779]] Wikidata name: Kifune Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q276779]] Wikidata name: Kifune Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:10, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.12166667|N|135.76277778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''{{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|WD=Q667783|lt=Sandō}}'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that {{ill|Hashihime|en|Hashihime|WD=Q3783599|lt=Hashihime}} (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni}}'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh}} play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>
== See also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== * [http://kifunejinja.jp/ Official Site] * {{Twitter}}

==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that {{ill|Hashihime|en|Hashihime|WD=Q3783599|lt=Hashihime}} (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni}}'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh}} play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.12166667|N|135.76277778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320}} }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''{{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|WD=Q667783|lt=Sandō}}'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that {{ill|Hashihime|en|Hashihime|WD=Q3783599|lt=Hashihime}} (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni}}'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh}} play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>
== See also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== * [http://kifunejinja.jp/ Official Site] * {{Twitter}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. {{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.12166667|N|135.76277778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Kuraokami|WD=Q644201|1=Kuraokami}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''{{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|WD=Q667783|lt=Sandō|1=Sandō}}'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that {{ill|Hashihime|en|Hashihime|WD=Q3783599|lt=Hashihime|1=Hashihime}} (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni|1=Oni}}'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}} play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>
== See also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]

==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]

From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that {{ill|Hashihime|en|Hashihime|WD=Q3783599|lt=Hashihime|1=Hashihime}} (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni|1=Oni}}'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}} play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>
== See also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = '''Kifune Shrine''' | native_name = {{Nihongo|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} | native_name_lang = | image = File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.12166667|N|135.76277778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Kuraokami|qid=Q644201|1=Kuraokami}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Sakyō-ku|qid=Q1321832|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|qid=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}; {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} }} [[File:貴船神社 Kibune Jinja (KYOTO-JAPAN) (4951368080).jpg|thumb|The ''{{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|qid=Q667783|lt=Sandō|1=Sandō}}'' at Kifune Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''Kifune Shrine'''|貴船神社|Kifune Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|qid=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These [[gohei|heihaku]] were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Kifune Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Kifune Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣中社|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
The shrine is also associated with the [[Ushi no toki mairi]], the ritual of wearing candles on one's head and laying a curse at a shrine during the "hour of the [[Ox (zodiac)|Ox]]", since it is from the resident deity that {{ill|Hashihime|en|Hashihime|qid=Q3783599|lt=Hashihime|1=Hashihime}} (Princess of the Uji Bridge) learns the prescribed ritual to turn herself into an ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|qid=Q730052|lt=Oni|1=Oni}}'' demon to exact vengeance, the story of which is immortalized in the {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|qid=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}} play ''Kanawa'' ("The Iron Crown").<ref>{{Cite book|last=Wakita|first=Haruko|title=Women in medieval Japan: motherhood, household management and sexuality|publisher=Monash Asia Institute|year=2006|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MO-yAAAAIAAJ|page=30|isbn=4130270338}}</ref>
== See also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
